pattern: Raise every base starting at 1 to the xth power until it exceeds n to get a small pool of usable values, then this becomes ordinary 0/1 subset-sum counting: how many subsets of that pool add up to exactly n.
hints: Only bases whose xth power does not exceed n can ever be used, and that pool shrinks fast as x grows -- generate it first.
Once you have the pool of powers, the question is identical to counting subsets of a list of numbers that sum to a target, which is a classic 0/1 knapsack counting DP.

Build a 1-D array dp of size n+1 with dp[0] = 1, and for each power (processed one at a time, iterating the sum downward) add dp[s - power] into dp[s] so every power is used at most once; the answer is dp[n] modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: smallest possible n and largest exponent
cube pool with a mid-sized target
maximum n with exponent 1, largest subset-sum pool
maximum n with the largest exponent, tiny pool
no combination of 5th powers can reach this n
only the value 1 is available and it cannot be reused, so this n is unreachable
n itself is exactly one base's power, plus nothing else fits

stress.description: random n and exponent across the full constraint range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 300)))
    x = rng.randint(1, 5)
    return [n, x]
